title: Microsoft to spend $25bn on Australian AI blitz
full_text: Other Editions 24 April 2026 MarketWatch Microsoft to spend $25bn on Australian AI blitz The Australian Jared Lynch 24 April 2026 1395 words English AUSTLN 23 © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. Microsoft chief executive Satya Nadella is positioning artificial ­intelligence as the great hope for reversing Australia’s flatlining productivity, while acknowledging the widespread fear that it will also bring significant job “displacement”. In an interview with The Australian, Mr Nadella announced Microsoft will spend $25bn in Australia – its biggest investment in the country – in the next three years and expand its AI supercomputing and cloud infrastructure. The company has also pledged to train about three million Australians. The capital injection is on top of the $5bn Microsoft is spending on building nine new data centres in Australia, and a part of a broader global bet that the productivity dividends from AI will ultimately justify the extraordinary cost of building necessary infrastructure. A portion of the $25bn will also help strengthen the nation’s online defences via a partnership with the Australian Signals Directorate. It comes amid heightened geopolitical tensions from the conflict in Iran, which has sparked a global energy crisis and a sharp rise in cyber attacks. Mr Nadella, one of the world’s most powerful tech bosses, said he “can’t change the world; I have to meet the world as is”. “The geopolitics of the world are defined by a set of forces that, in some sense, are independent of everything else that’s maybe happening, whether they are economic forces or technological forces,” Mr Nadella said. “But ultimately, I think whether it’s for companies or for countries, it is to figure out how to benefit using the things that we can control … make sure that we earn permission, quite frankly, to operate one community, one country at a time, by making sure that this is not just technology for technology’s sake, but it is to a means to some broader, bigger end. “And I think that is, quite frankly, what the purpose of any business is.” Defining what a good company is in the age of AI is a key theme for Mr Nadella. He said the AI revolution would trigger job losses, acknowledging that “displacement” will be an inevitable part of the shift but society will be better off in the long run. “It’s not just displacement for displacement sake. It’s about innovation and change to ultimately help the society move forward and build that wealth,” he said. Microsoft laid off 15,000 staff last year as part of a shift to become a more AI-centric company. Atlassian, Afterpay owner Block and WiseTech Global are also sacking thousands, citing AI disruption in what has been dubbed the ‘SaaSpocalypse’. Microsoft’s Australian investment is focused on deploying advanced AI processors and enhancing local Azure supercomputing capacity to capitalise on the agentic or autonomous AI era. “People talk about what’s happening to software. Guess what? All software is going agentic. And you need to host these agents. You need to have them have their own computer, essentially,” Mr Nadella said. It comes amid a period of intense financial scrutiny on Big Tech’s AI build-out. Investors have expressed rising anxiety over the staggering capital expenditures required to win the AI race, with Microsoft shares falling more than 20 per cent from their peak last October. This is despite Mr Nadella attempting to temper hype, stating recently that AI capabilities have so far “outpaced real-world impact" and that adoption must spread beyond the technology sector to avoid what he has termed “a financial bubble”. Investor concern centres on the possibility that tech giants are overspending without sufficient proof that the colossal demand for AI services will remain both steady and lucrative. Microsoft ploughed $US37.5bn ($52.5bn) into capital expenditures in the three months to December 31. The company banked a net income of $US38.5bn, translating to $US5.16 per diluted share, for the period. ChatGPT creator OpenAI drives 45 per cent of Microsoft’s remaining cloud commitments. It also plans to spend $US10bn in Japan during the next four years to, as in Australia, further expand AI and cybersecurity infrastructure. Mr Nadella said that this vast spending spree is necessary to catalyse an economic shift, particularly in markets like Australia, where productivity has been stagnant. “Australia has an enormous opportunity to translate AI into real economic growth and societal benefit,” Mr Nadella said. His strategy involves a complex negotiation, balancing a massive, uncertain capital outlay with the promised nonlinear economic gains, while managing the societal fears of job disruption through large-scale skilling and national security collaboration. He visited Australia this week for the first time in more than six years to speak with the company’s biggest customers, including Commonwealth Bank, Telstra, Australia Post, and even Cricket Australia, at Microsoft’s annual AI tour. Mr Nadella describes digital technology as a “valuable input” whose price drops consistently due to Moore’s Law and the “malleability of software”. He called software the first “commodity” discovered by humankind with “essentially a massive deflationary pressure to it”. The challenge, he said, is how to use this cheap, powerful input to “create output that is valuable in the world”. Mr Nadella cited examples of this translating into “social surplus and economic surplus", such as university teachers using cognitive tools to make lesson plans more engaging and the Australian Energy Market Operator leveraging AI to better manage complex grids. “That’s productivity. And so to me, that’s – I think it's right – which is for Australia or for any country or for any company, being able to use the power of this new technology to take the outcome that they are in the business of generating … and improve that non-linearly even with technology,” he said. Mr Nadella said that the “ultimate test” of the $25bn Australian investment is tied to economic benefits beyond Microsoft. “It’s not about just our $25bn investment; it’s about the economic value it creates around us,” he said. “It’s the economic opportunity we create for small, small start-ups; public sector efficiency; health outcomes; education outcomes; and large multinationals out of here that become more competitive because of our technology input. “And so to me, yes, it’s a big investment. We’re thrilled to make it. But ultimately, the test here is for us to make sure that we stay grounded in the fact that this investment has to translate not just into ROIC (return on invested capital) for us, but fundamentally into ROIC for the Australian companies, in terms of them using AI to drive outcomes that they care about.” The sheer scale of the investment – which is underpinned by a memorandum of understanding with the Australian government regarding expectations for AI infrastructure developers – signals Microsoft’s strategy to embed itself as a key enabler of the country’s economic and security architecture. Microsoft has pledged to train three million Australians with workforce-ready AI skills by 2028. The company is extending its efforts into the education system, launching programs like Elevate for Educators and a partnership with youth career coaching platform Anyway, formerly known as Year 13, to bring a free AI-powered Career Coach to up to 1000 Australian schools. These social initiatives are designed to function as an integral part of Microsoft’s “permission to operate” in Australia by creating economic opportunity, public sector efficiency, and improved health and education outcomes. The $25bn investment also includes strengthening Australia’s national cyber defence. Microsoft will expand the Microsoft-Australian Signals Directorate Cyber Shield (MACS) to cover additional federal agencies. Microsoft will expand its collaboration with the ASD, Home Affairs and the Digital Transformation Agency to protect Australia’s most critical government systems and infrastructure. Since inception, the MACS program has already secured more than 38,000 government accounts, identified 35 previously unknown vulnerabilities, and delivered a bespoke engineering solution with Microsoft Sentinel, allowing customers to more easily integrate into the government’s Cyber Threat Intelligence Sharing program. Anthony Albanese said: “We want to make sure all Australians benefit from AI. “Our National AI Plan is all about capturing the economic opportunities of this transformative technology while protecting Australians from the risks. Microsoft’s long-term investment in our national capability will help deliver on that plan – strengthening our cyber defences and creating opportunity for Australian workers and businesses.” The Australian-20260424-Australian-23-000500359613 Nationwide News Pty Ltd. summary: Microsoft plans to invest $25 billion in Australia over the next three years to enhance AI capabilities and infrastructure, aiming to boost productivity amid concerns about job displacement. The investment includes training for approximately three million Australians and strengthening cybersecurity in partnership with the Australian Signals Directorate. CEO Satya Nadella emphasised the need for balancing technological advancement with societal impacts and economic growth.
publication:
Microsoft to spend $25bn on Australian AI blitz
The Australian24 April 20261,395 wordsJared Lynch

Microsoft chief executive Satya Nadella is positioning artificial ­intelligence as the great hope for reversing Australia’s flatlining productivity, while acknowledging the widespread fear that it will also bring significant job ...
key_points: Microsoft's $25 billion investment focuses on AI and cloud infrastructure in Australia.
The company aims to train three million Australians and improve cybersecurity.
Nadella acknowledges job displacement but believes AI will ultimately benefit society.
relevance: This investment highlights the intersection of technology and economic growth, which is crucial for actuaries and financial professionals in understanding market trends and workforce impacts.
